claims.claim: PCL* learns behavioral automata from stochastic mock LLMs with ≤40 states and ≥92% prediction accuracy
evidence: Phase 0 Python experiments: 4 model×property combos (phase0_experiments.py)
Results: 19-40 states, 92.0-100.0% accuracy (phase0_results.json)
Runnable: python3 phase0_experiments.py

claims.claim: PCL* convergence: d_TV(M_α, H) ≤ ε with probability ≥ 1-δ in Õ(β·n₀·log(1/δ)) queries
evidence: Theorem 1 in tool_paper.tex with proof sketch in Appendix A.1
Causal coupling caveat: Remark 4.3 in tool_paper.tex addresses sequential conditioning vs naive union bound
Regularity conditions explicitly stated: bounded outputs, local stationarity, independent sampling
Property-based tests: pclstar_hoeffding_bound, pclstar_query_complexity_monotone in property_tests.rs
Convergence bounds implementation: caber-core/src/learning/convergence.rs
assumptions: Local stationarity (monitored via CUSUM drift detection)
caveats: Union bound over causally coupled errors yields conservative but sound guarantee (Remark 4.3)
Sequential conditioning analysis provided as tighter alternative

claims.claim: Specification soundness exceeds 96% for models up to 100 states
evidence: Mock model experiments: 98.3% (n₀≤50), 96.7% (n₀=100), 94.2% (n₀=200) (tool_paper.tex Table 4)

claims.claim: Bisimulation distances track behavioral drift within 9% relative error
evidence: Table 5 in tool_paper.tex: relative error 3.1-8.9% across 8 configurations
Property-based tests: bisim_distance_reflexive, bisim_distance_symmetric, bisim_distance_bounded, differential_test_bisim_distance_known in property_tests.rs
Remark on ε-behavioral equivalence vs approximate bisimulation in §3.5

claims.claim: Functor bandwidth grows sublinearly in alphabet size
evidence: Table 3 in tool_paper.tex: β grows from 3.1 (n=10) to 14.2 (n=200) vs naive alphabet 500
Figure 4 in tool_paper.tex: pgfplots visualization of β vs n₀
Property-based tests: functor_bandwidth_sublinear, functor_bandwidth_monotone in property_tests.rs
caveats: Estimating β itself is a hard problem (Remark 4.6); practical estimation uses greedy set-cover heuristic that yields upper bound

claims.claim: CoalCEGAR monotonicity: safety properties preserved under abstraction refinement
evidence: Proposition 1 in tool_paper.tex with proof sketch in Appendix A.3
Galois connection details in Appendix F
Liveness caveat explicitly stated
Property-based tests: coalcegar_safety_monotonicity, coalcegar_galois_distortion, coalcegar_refinement_reduces_distance in property_tests.rs
Galois connection implementation: caber-core/src/abstraction/galois.rs

claims.claim: Stochastic mock LLM behavioral surfaces admit tractable finite-state abstraction
evidence: Phase 0 validation: 4 stochastic mock LLMs with 3-6 ground-truth states all learned with ≤40 states
Figure 2 in tool_paper.tex: prediction accuracy bar chart
Runnable: python3 phase0_experiments.py
Results: phase0_results.json
claims.limitation: Demonstrated on stochastic mock LLMs (Markov chains), not real LLM APIs. Mock models are designed to be realistic but cannot capture the full complexity of deployed models.

claims.claim: Real LLM behavior admits tractable finite-state abstraction (gpt-4.1-nano)
evidence: Real LLM experiment: gpt-4.1-nano via OpenAI API, 63 total queries
Extracted 3-state behavioral automaton (initial, compliant, refusal)
3 behavioral properties verified: refusal persistence (3/3), sycophancy resistance (3/3), jailbreak resistance (3/3)
Runnable: python3 phase_b_experiments.py (requires OPENAI_API_KEY)
Results: phase_b_results.json
claims.limitation: gpt-4.1-nano is a small model with limited behavioral complexity (2 atoms). Only 3 trials per condition. Keyword-based classifier is coarse.

claims.claim: CABER produces 5-10x more compact automata than PDFA+PRISM baseline with higher accuracy
evidence: PDFA baseline comparison on 4 mock LLMs: CABER avg 30 states vs PDFA avg 293 states
CABER avg accuracy 90.1% vs PDFA avg 68.5% (21.6pp difference)
Table in tool_paper.tex §7.3
Runnable: python3 phase_b_experiments.py
Results: phase_b_results.json → pdfa_baseline_comparison

claims.claim: PCL* maintains ≥96% accuracy up to 100 ground-truth states with O(n^1.42) query scaling
evidence: State scaling experiment: 9 configurations from 3 to 100 ground-truth states
All achieve ≥96.4% prediction accuracy
Log-linear regression yields exponent 1.42, consistent with theoretical O~(n^{3/2})
Runnable: python3 phase_b_experiments.py
Results: phase_b_results.json → state_scaling_analysis

claims.claim: Pipeline maintains ≥99% verdict accuracy under 20% classifier error
evidence: Monte Carlo simulation: 2,000 trials per error rate (classifier_robustness_analysis.py)
Results: classifier_robustness_results.json
Figure 3 in tool_paper.tex: verdict accuracy vs classifier error rate
Theoretical bound: ε_total ≤ ε_learn/(1-ρ) + ε_mc + ρ (Equation 1, proof in Appendix A.2)
Causal coupling note in Appendix A.2: bound valid for state-independent errors, uses max error rate for correlated errors
Property-based tests: pac_total_error_decomposition, classifier_error_at_most_linear, sample_complexity_scaling in property_tests.rs
caber-integration/tests/property_tests.rs: 38 tests using proptest framework

Covers: bisimulation metrics, automaton properties, certificates, refusal persistence, paraphrase invariance, drift detection, union bounds, PAC error propagation, functor bandwidth, PCL* convergence, CoalCEGAR correctness, differential testing
Run: cargo test -p caber-integration --test property_tests

claims.claim: QCTL_F alternation-free fragment is polynomial-time decidable
evidence: Appendix C in tool_paper.tex: decidability analysis
All six specification templates fall within the alternation-free fragment
Fixed-point computation: caber-core/src/model_checker/fixpoint.rs

claims.claim: QCTL_F is an instantiation of the Pattinson-Schröder coalgebraic modal logic framework
evidence: Explicitly stated in §5 and §8 of tool_paper.tex
Contribution is the integration with learned automata and specification template library, not logical novelty

claims.claim: Classifier error bound: ε_total ≤ ε_learn/(1-ρ) + ε_mc + ρ
evidence: Equation 1 in tool_paper.tex
Proof in Appendix A.2 with causal coupling caveat
Sensitivity analysis data: classifier_robustness_results.json (Appendix D, Table B.1)
Property-based tests: pac_total_error_decomposition in property_tests.rs

claims.claim: Query cost: ~$200-$500 per audit at current API pricing
evidence: Computed from Phase 0 query counts (71K-94K queries × ~100 tokens × $30-60/M tokens)
Discussed in tool_paper.tex Section 7 (Evaluation)

claims.claim: Sub-distribution Kantorovich bounds hold with same rates as full distributions
evidence: Remark 4.5 in tool_paper.tex explains sub-distribution treatment
Missing mass transported to sink state at unit cost (Baldan et al. 2018)

claims.claim: Cosine similarity converted to angular distance for metric axiom compliance
evidence: Appendix H in tool_paper.tex: angular distance formula and metric verification

claims.claim: AALpy+PRISM comparison is structurally different from CABER
evidence: Comparison caveat explicitly stated in §7.1 of tool_paper.tex
Baseline provided with manually defined alphabet; CABER discovers its own

not_claimed: Lean 4 formalization of core theorems (acknowledged as future work, estimated 80-120K LoC; explicitly stated as not claimed in paper §9)
Frontier LLM validation (Phase B uses gpt-4.1-nano; GPT-4/Claude/Llama validation identified as next step in §9)
Production deployment (tool is research prototype; query cost limitations discussed in §8.5)
Hyperproperties or fairness specifications (QCTL_F excludes these)
Functorial alphabet abstraction (non-functoriality acknowledged as limitation in §8.5)
Logical novelty of QCTL_F (explicitly positioned as Pattinson-Schröder instantiation)
Adversarial robustness of embedding model (acknowledged as open vulnerability in §8.5)
honest_limitations: Real LLM validation uses gpt-4.1-nano (a small model); frontier model validation (GPT-4, Claude, Llama) is future work
PDFA baseline comparison uses simplified ALERGIA + value iteration, not the full AALpy+PRISM pipeline
State scaling analysis caps at max_states parameter; very large models (>100 states) may need hierarchical learning

Alphabet abstraction (embedding clustering) is non-functorial: clustering depends on samples, breaks compositionality. Mitigated by large sample sets and silhouette monitoring. Presheaf fix deferred to future work.
Convergence theorem assumes local stationarity, which deployed LLMs may violate due to silent model updates. CUSUM drift monitoring detects gross violations.
Classifier errors are bounded (Equation 1) but not eliminated: at ρ=0.20, error bound degrades from 0.05 to ~0.27.
No formal verification (Lean 4 estimated at 80-120K LoC); 38 property-based tests bridge the gap computationally
QCTL_F is an instantiation of existing coalgebraic modal logic, not a novel logic. Contribution is integration with learned automata and specification templates.
Embedding model alignment with behavioral equivalence is unvalidated on adversarial inputs
ML-verification boundary is conditional: formal guarantees assume ML-constructed abstraction is correct
Functor bandwidth estimation is itself computationally hard; practical estimate is an upper bound via greedy heuristic

paper_to_artifact_mapping.Theorem 1 (PCL* Convergence): Proof sketch: tool_paper.tex Appendix A.1; Remark 4.3 (causal coupling); Implementation: caber-core/src/learning/convergence.rs; Tests: pclstar_hoeffding_bound, pclstar_query_complexity_monotone
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Theorem 2 (Bandwidth-Sample Complexity): Full proof: tool_paper.tex §4.3; Connection to metric entropy (Kolmogorov-Tikhomirov); Empirical validation: Table 3; Implementation: caber-core/src/coalgebra/bandwidth.rs; Tests: functor_bandwidth_sublinear, functor_bandwidth_monotone
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Theorem 3 (End-to-End Error Composition): Theorem: tool_paper.tex §7.2; Full proof: Appendix A.4; Decomposes 5 error sources; Numerical example: total ≤ 0.173; Consistent with empirical >92% accuracy
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Remark 4.3 (Causal Coupling): Sequential conditioning argument addressing union bound critique; tool_paper.tex §4.1
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Remark 4.8 (CoalCEGAR Termination): Finite lattice argument; 2-4 refinement steps in practice
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Equation 1 (Classifier Error): Proof: tool_paper.tex Appendix A.2 with causal coupling note; Validation: classifier_robustness_analysis.py → classifier_robustness_results.json; Tests: pac_total_error_decomposition
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Proposition 1 (CoalCEGAR Monotonicity): Proof sketch: tool_paper.tex Appendix A.3; Galois connection details: Appendix F; Implementation: caber-core/src/abstraction/galois.rs; Tests: coalcegar_safety_monotonicity
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Section 7.4 (PDFA+PRISM Separation): Three structural advantages argued: functor-parameterized abstraction, quantitative bisimulation, property-indexed complexity; AALpy+PRISM baseline comparison with caveats
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Definition 2 (QCTL_F Syntax): Positioned as Pattinson-Schröder instantiation in §5; Implementation: caber-core/src/temporal/syntax.rs
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Definition 3 (Functor Bandwidth): Formal definition via ε-covering numbers; Connection to metric entropy; Implementation: caber-core/src/coalgebra/bandwidth.rs; Tests: functor_bandwidth_sublinear, functor_bandwidth_monotone
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Table 4 (Specification Soundness): Mock model experiments in tool_paper.tex; comparison caveat noted for AALpy+PRISM baseline
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Figure 1 (Architecture): Code organization matches: caber-core/ layers 1-6, caber-python/ layer 7
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Appendix F (Galois Connection): Detailed abstraction/concretization maps and distortion bounds
paper_to_artifact_mapping.Appendix G (Cosine to Angular): Angular distance metric for Kantorovich lifting
